title: Hire a temporary worker through the Seasonal Agricultural Worker Program - Overview - Canada.ca
content:
## Hire a temporary worker through the Seasonal Agricultural Worker Program: Overview
- 2. Program requirements
- 3. Wages, working conditions and occupations
- 4. Recruitment and advertisement
- 5. Apply for a Labour Market Impact Assessment
The Seasonal Agricultural Worker Program (SAWP) allows employers to hire temporary foreign workers when Canadians and permanent residents aren't available.
These employers can hire temporary foreign workers from participating countries for a maximum period of 8 months, between January 1 and December 15, provided they’re able to offer the workers a minimum of 240 hours of work within a period of 6 weeks or less.
To qualify for the SAWP, employers must  meet 3 criteria:
- the temporary foreign workers hired must be citizens  from Mexico or participating Caribbean countries
- production must be in specific  commodity sectors
- the activity must be related to  on farm primary agriculture

### Participating countries
The SAWP applies only to temporary foreign workers who are  citizens from:
- Caribbean countries of: Anguilla Antigua and Barbuda Barbados Dominica Grenada Jamaica Montserrat St. Kitts-Nevis St. Lucia St. Vincent and the Grenadines Trinidad and Tobago
- St. Vincent and the Grenadines

### Role of participating foreign governments
The SAWP operates according to bilateral  agreements between Canada and the participating countries. The agreements  outline the role of these foreign governments, which is to:
- recruit and select the temporary foreign workers
- make sure workers have the  necessary documents
- maintain a pool of qualified  workers, and
- appoint representatives to  assist workers in Canada
These governments also ensure that the men  and women selected to work temporarily in Canada meet all the requirements of  the SAWP. These requirements include being:
- experienced in farming
- at least 18 years of age
- a citizen of one of the  participating countries, and
- able to satisfy the: Canadian immigration laws, and laws of the worker’s home  country
- Canadian immigration laws, and
- laws of the worker’s home  country

### National commodity list
- Fruits, vegetables (including canning/processing of these products if grown on the farm)
- Grains, oil seeds and seed corn
- Nursery-grown trees (including Christmas trees, greenhouses/nurseries)
- Pedigreed canola seed

### Give feedback about this page
Thank you for your feedback.
